| Call for Proposals This Group encourages the submission of any individual paper or panel proposals that make cultural and moral diversity central to ethical analysis. Themes especially welcome this year include:  * Environmental crises, whether the result of natural or human causes  * Contemporary religious protest movements  * Economic justice Mission While comparative assessment of the ethics of different religious groups is an ancient and widespread pursuit, the modern field of comparative religious ethics arguably dates from the founding of the *Journal of Religious Ethics* in 1973. (For the purposes of this statement, âethicsâ as a subject will refer to reflection about how best to live as human beings; an âethicâ is one more or less determinate position on the best mode(s) of life). While there have been a variety of motivations for the attempt to study âreligious ethicsâ rather than or in addition to âChristian ethics,â one animating idea has been the growing recognition that people from numerous religions propound sophisticated and powerful moral visions, which possess intriguing similarities and differences and are not easily reducible to a common denominator. In addition, the variety and particular characteristics of such visions are historically and politically significant in the modern era of increasingly pervasive globalization. Indeed, comparative ethics may be desperately needed in our contemporary context of global interdependence, misunderstanding, and mutual mistrust. There are thus ample grounds, both social and purely intellectual, to suggest that this ethical variety needs to be engaged directly via rigorous comparison. Comparative ethics makes such diversity central to its analysis, which includes three main aspects:  * Describes and interprets particular ethics on the basis of historical, anthropological, or other data  * Compares such ethics (in the plural) and requires searching reflection on the methods and tools of inquiry  * Engages in normative argument on the basis of such studies, and may thereby speak to contemporary concerns about overlapping identities, cultural complexity and plurality, universalism and relativism, and political problems regarding the coexistence of divergent social groups, as well as particular moral controversies.  Ideally, each of these aspects enriches the others; for example, comparison across traditions helps generate more insightful interpretations of particular figures and themes. This self-conscious sophistication about differing ethical vocabularies and the analytical practices necessary to grapple with them is what makes comparative ethics distinctive within broader conversations in religious and philosophical ethics. Comparative ethics as envisioned here induces conversation across typical area studies boundaries by involving scholars of different religions, and all sessions in this Group are constructed with this goal in mind, so that data from multiple traditions will be brought to bear on any comparative theme. # Call for Proposals

This Group encourages the submission of any individual paper or panel proposals that make cultural and moral diversity central to ethical analysis. Themes especially welcome this year include:

* Environmental crises, whether the result of natural or human causes

* Contemporary religious protest movements

* Economic justice

- Contemporary religious protest movements

- Economic justice

- Economic justice

# Mission

While comparative assessment of the ethics of different religious groups is an ancient and widespread pursuit, the modern field of comparative religious ethics arguably dates from the founding of the *Journal of Religious Ethics* in 1973. (For the purposes of this statement, âethicsâ as a subject will refer to reflection about how best to live as human beings; an âethicâ is one more or less determinate position on the best mode(s) of life). While there have been a variety of motivations for the attempt to study âreligious ethicsâ rather than or in addition to âChristian ethics,â one animating idea has been the growing recognition that people from numerous religions propound sophisticated and powerful moral visions, which possess intriguing similarities and differences and are not easily reducible to a common denominator. In addition, the variety and particular characteristics of such visions are historically and politically significant in the modern era of increasingly pervasive globalization. Indeed, comparative ethics may be desperately needed in our contemporary context of global interdependence, misunderstanding, and mutual mistrust. There are thus ample grounds, both social and purely intellectual, to suggest that this ethical variety needs to be engaged directly via rigorous comparison. Comparative ethics makes such diversity central to its analysis, which includes three main aspects:

* Describes and interprets particular ethics on the basis of historical, anthropological, or other data

* Compares such ethics (in the plural) and requires searching reflection on the methods and tools of inquiry

* Engages in normative argument on the basis of such studies, and may thereby speak to contemporary concerns about overlapping identities, cultural complexity and plurality, universalism and relativism, and political problems regarding the coexistence of divergent social groups, as well as particular moral controversies.

- Compares such ethics (in the plural) and requires searching reflection on the methods and tools of inquiry

- Engages in normative argument on the basis of such studies, and may thereby speak to contemporary concerns about overlapping identities, cultural complexity and plurality, universalism and relativism, and political problems regarding the coexistence of divergent social groups, as well as particular moral controversies.

- Engages in normative argument on the basis of such studies, and may thereby speak to contemporary concerns about overlapping identities, cultural complexity and plurality, universalism and relativism, and political problems regarding the coexistence of divergent social groups, as well as particular moral controversies.

Ideally, each of these aspects enriches the others; for example, comparison across traditions helps generate more insightful interpretations of particular figures and themes. This self-conscious sophistication about differing ethical vocabularies and the analytical practices necessary to grapple with them is what makes comparative ethics distinctive within broader conversations in religious and philosophical ethics. Comparative ethics as envisioned here induces conversation across typical area studies boundaries by involving scholars of different religions, and all sessions in this Group are constructed with this goal in mind, so that data from multiple traditions will be brought to bear on any comparative theme.

# Anonymity of Review Process

Proposer names are **visible** to Chairs but **anonymous** to steering committee members.

# Questions?

Elizabeth Bucar  
University of North Carolina, Greensboro

Irene Oh  
George Washington University

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Queue.svg/100px-Queue.svg.png)

**Comparative religion** is the branch of the [study of religions](/wiki/Study_of_religions "Study of religions") with the systematic comparison of the [doctrines](/wiki/Doctrines "Doctrines") and practices, themes and impacts (including [migration](/wiki/Human_migration "Human migration")) of the world's [religions](/wiki/Religion "Religion"). In general the comparative study of religion yields a deeper understanding of the fundamental philosophical concerns of religion such as [ethics](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics"), [metaphysics](/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics") and the nature and forms of [salvation](/wiki/Salvation "Salvation"). It also considers and compares the origins and [similarities](/wiki/Similarity_(philosophy) "Similarity (philosophy)") shared between the various religions of the world. Studying such material facilitates a broadened and more sophisticated understanding of human [beliefs](/wiki/Beliefs "Beliefs") and practices regarding the [sacred](/wiki/Sacred "Sacred"), [numinous](/wiki/Numinous "Numinous"), [spiritual](/wiki/Spirituality "Spirituality") and [divine](/wiki/Divinity "Divinity").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

In the field of comparative religion, a common geographical classification[[2]](#cite_note-EB-2) of the [main world religions](/wiki/Major_religious_groups "Major religious groups") distinguishes groups such as [Middle Eastern religions](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Middle_East "Religion in the Middle East") (including [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religions "Abrahamic religions") and [Iranian religions](/wiki/Iranian_religions "Iranian religions")), [Indian religions](/wiki/Indian_religions "Indian religions"), [East Asian religions](/wiki/East_Asian_religions "East Asian religions"), [African religions](/wiki/African_religions "African religions"), [American religions](/wiki/Native_American_religion "Native American religion"), [Oceanic religions](/wiki/Religion_in_Oceania "Religion in Oceania"), and [classical](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") [Hellenistic religions](/wiki/Hellenistic_religion "Hellenistic religion").[[2]](#cite_note-EB-2)

There also exist various [sociological classifications of religious movements](/wiki/Sociological_classifications_of_religious_movements "Sociological classifications of religious movements").

## History

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b0/Monumento_a_Ibn_Hazm.jpg)

[Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni") (973 – c. 1050) and [Ibn Hazm](/wiki/Ibn_Hazm "Ibn Hazm") (994 – 1064), both of the [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age") and considered the "fathers of comparative religion", compared the study of religious pluralism and their works have been significant in the fields of theology and philosophy.[[3]](#cite_note-Arberry-3)[[4]](#cite_note-brill-4)[[5]](#cite_note-gulf-5)[[6]](#cite_note-hadithsunnah-6) Al-Biruni discussed his idea of history in *[The Remaining Signs of Past Centuries](/wiki/The_Remaining_Signs_of_Past_Centuries "The Remaining Signs of Past Centuries")* (c. 1000 AD) which was translated by [Eduard Sachau](/wiki/Eduard_Sachau "Eduard Sachau") in the 19th century. It is a comparative study of calendars of different cultures and civilizations, interlaced with mathematical, astronomical, and historical information, exploring the customs and religions of different peoples.[[7]](#cite_note-Khan-7)

In the seventeenth century, antiquarians such as [Athanasius Kircher](/wiki/Athanasius_Kircher "Athanasius Kircher") along with [Sir Thomas Browne](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_Browne "Sir Thomas Browne") were pioneering scholars of comparative religion.

Social scientists in the 19th century took a strong interest in comparative and "primitive" religion through the work of [Max Müller](/wiki/Max_M%C3%BCller "Max Müller"), [Edward Burnett Tylor](/wiki/Edward_Burnett_Tylor "Edward Burnett Tylor"), [William Robertson Smith](/wiki/William_Robertson_Smith "William Robertson Smith"), [James George Frazer](/wiki/James_George_Frazer "James George Frazer"), [Émile Durkheim](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Durkheim "Émile Durkheim"), [Max Weber](/wiki/Max_Weber "Max Weber"), and [Rudolf Otto](/wiki/Rudolf_Otto "Rudolf Otto").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)
[Nicholas de Lange](/wiki/Nicholas_de_Lange "Nicholas de Lange"), Professor of Hebrew and Jewish Studies at [Cambridge University](/wiki/Cambridge_University "Cambridge University"), says that

The comparative study of religions is an academic discipline which has been developed within Christian theology faculties, and it has a tendency to force widely differing phenomena into a kind of strait-jacket cut to a Christian pattern. The problem is not only that other 'religions' may have little or nothing to say about questions which are of burning importance for Christianity, but that they may not even see themselves as religions in precisely the same way in which Christianity sees itself as a religion.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Examples that demonstrate this point are Buddhism and Chinese Folk Religions. These belief systems have not historically been seen as mutually exclusive and have blended over time into different beliefs such as [Pure Land Buddhism](/wiki/Pure_Land_Buddhism "Pure Land Buddhism"). This shows a marked difference from Western conceptions of religions, which see adherence to one religion as precluding membership of another faith.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Hinduism and Buddhism provide another insight in the form of [soteriology](/wiki/Soteriology "Soteriology"). Comparative study of religions may approach religions with a base idea of salvation with eternal life after death, but religions like Hinduism or Buddhism don't necessarily share this view. Instead, Hinduism and [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") Buddhism both speak of a falling back into nonexistence and escaping the cycle of [reincarnation](/wiki/Reincarnation "Reincarnation"), rather than eternal life after death.[[12]](#cite_note-:3-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Geographical classification

According to [Charles Joseph Adams](/wiki/Charles_Joseph_Adams "Charles Joseph Adams"), in the field of comparative religion, a common geographical classification discerns[[2]](#cite_note-EB-2) the [main world religions](/wiki/Major_religious_groups "Major religious groups") as follows:[[2]](#cite_note-EB-2)

### Middle Eastern religions

#### Abrahamic or Western Asian religions

In the study of comparative religion, the category of [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religion "Abrahamic religion") includes the three [monotheistic](/wiki/Monotheistic "Monotheistic") religions, [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), which claim [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham") (Hebrew *Avraham* אַבְרָהָם; Arabic *Ibrahim* إبراهيم ) as a part of their sacred history. Smaller religions such as the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") that fit this description are sometimes included but are often omitted.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The original belief in the [God of Abraham](/wiki/God_in_Abrahamic_religions "God in Abrahamic religions") eventually became strictly monotheistic present-day [Rabbinic Judaism](/wiki/Rabbinic_Judaism "Rabbinic Judaism"). Religious Jews regard Judaism as the expression of the [covenant](/wiki/Mosaic_covenant "Mosaic covenant") that [God](/wiki/God_in_Judaism "God in Judaism") established with the [Children of Israel](/wiki/Children_of_Israel "Children of Israel"). Jews hold that the [Torah](/wiki/Torah "Torah") forms part of the larger text known as the [Tanakh](/wiki/Tanakh "Tanakh") or the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible"). They also look to a supplemental oral tradition represented by later texts such as the [Midrash](/wiki/Midrash "Midrash") and the [Talmud](/wiki/Talmud "Talmud").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Christians believe that Christianity represents the [fulfillment](/wiki/Supersessionism "Supersessionism") and continuation of the Jewish [Old Testament](/wiki/Tanakh "Tanakh"). Christians believe that [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") (Hebrew *[Yeshua](/wiki/Yeshua "Yeshua")* יֵשׁוּעַ) is the awaited [Messiah](/wiki/Messiah "Messiah") (Christ) foretold in the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") [prophecies](/wiki/Prophecy "Prophecy"), and also honor the subsequently produced [New Testament scriptures](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Generally, Christians believe that Jesus is both the [incarnation](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)") of God and the [Son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God "Son of God"). Their [creeds](/wiki/Creed "Creed") generally hold in common that the incarnation, [ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus"), [suffering](/wiki/Passion_of_Jesus "Passion of Jesus"), [death on the cross](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), and [resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") of Jesus took place for the [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity") of mankind.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Islam teaches that the present Christian and Jewish scriptures have been [corrupted](/wiki/Tahrif "Tahrif") over time and are no longer the original divine revelations as given to the [Jewish people](/wiki/Revelation#Mass_revelation "Revelation") and to [Moses](/wiki/Mosaic_authorship "Mosaic authorship"), [Jesus](/wiki/Pauline_Christianity "Pauline Christianity"), and other prophets. For Muslims, the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") is the final, complete revelation from [God](/wiki/God "God") (Arabic الله *[Allah](/wiki/Allah "Allah")*); they believe it to have been [revealed](/wiki/Divine_revelation "Divine revelation") to [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") alone, whom they characterise as the [final prophet of Islam](/wiki/Prophet_of_Islam "Prophet of Islam") and as the *[Khatam an-Nabiyyin](/wiki/Khatam_an-Nabiyyin "Khatam an-Nabiyyin")*, meaning the last of the prophets ever sent by Allah ("seal of the prophets").

Based on the Muslim figure of the [Mahdī](/wiki/Mahd%C4%AB "Mahdī"), the ultimate savior of humankind and the final Imām of the Twelve Imams, [Ali Muhammad Shirazi](/wiki/B%C3%A1b "Báb"), later known as "the Báb", created the Bábí movement out of the belief that he was the gate to the Twelfth Imām. This signaled a break with Islam and started a new religious system, [Bábism](/wiki/B%C3%A1bism "Bábism"). However, in the 1860s a split occurred, after which the vast majority of Bábís who considered Mirza Husayn `Ali or [Bahá'u'lláh](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%27u%27ll%C3%A1h "Bahá'u'lláh") to be the Báb's spiritual successor founded the Baháʼí Movement, while the minority who followed Subh-i-Azal came to be called [Azalis](/wiki/Azalis "Azalis").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The Baháʼí division eventually became a full-fledged religion in its own right, the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"). In comparison to the other Abrahamic religions - Judaism, Christianity and Islam - the number of adherents for Baháʼí faith and other minor Abrahamic religions are not very numerous.

Out of the three major Abrahamic faiths, [Christianity and Judaism](/wiki/Christianity_and_Judaism "Christianity and Judaism") are the two religions that diverge the most in theology and practice.

The historical interaction between [Islam and Judaism](/wiki/Islam_and_Judaism "Islam and Judaism") started in the 7th century CE with the origin and [spread of Islam](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam "Spread of Islam"). There are many common aspects between Islam and Judaism, and as Islam [developed](/wiki/History_of_Islam "History of Islam"), it gradually became the major religion closest to Judaism. In contrast to Christianity, which originated from interaction between ancient [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), [Roman](/wiki/Roman_empire "Roman empire"), and [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew "Hebrew") cultures, Judaism is very similar to Islam in its fundamental religious outlook, structure, jurisprudence and practice.[[19]](#cite_note-19) There are many traditions within Islam that originate from traditions which are recorded in the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") or which stem from post-biblical Jewish traditions. These practices are known collectively as the *[Isra'iliyat](/wiki/Isra%27iliyat "Isra'iliyat")*.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The historical interaction between [Christianity and Islam](/wiki/Christianity_and_Islam "Christianity and Islam") connects fundamental ideas in Christianity with similar ones in Islam. Islam accepts many aspects of Christianity as part of its faith – with some differences in interpretation – and rejects other aspects; for example, it simplifies complex Christian  [Christological](/wiki/Christology "Christology") teaching and completely avoids the potential pitfalls of [Trinitarianism](/wiki/Trinitarianism "Trinitarianism"). Islam holds that the Quran is the final revelation from [God](/wiki/God "God") and a completion of all previous revelations, including the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible").

[Mandaeism](/wiki/Mandaeism "Mandaeism"), sometimes also known as Sabianism (after the mysterious [Sabians](/wiki/Sabians "Sabians") mentioned in the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran"), a name historically claimed by several religious groups),[[21]](#cite_note-21) is a [Gnostic](/wiki/Gnosticism#Mandaeism "Gnosticism") and [monotheistic](/wiki/Monotheism#Mandaeism "Monotheism") religion.[[22]](#cite_note-Mandaens-22): 4[[23]](#cite_note-23) [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist") is their chief prophet, and frequent [baptism](/wiki/Baptism#Mandaean_Baptism "Baptism") is part of their core practices. According to most scholars, Mandaeism originated sometime in the first three centuries CE, either in southwestern [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") or in the [Syro-Palestinian area](/wiki/Levant "Levant").[[24]](#cite_note-auto-24) However, some scholars take the view that Mandaeism is older and dates from pre-Christian times.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Mandaeans assert that their religion predates Judaism, Christianity, and Islam as a monotheistic faith.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Mandaeans believe that they descend directly from [Shem](/wiki/Shem "Shem"), [Noah](/wiki/Noah "Noah")'s son,[[27]](#cite_note-auto2-27): 182 and also from John the Baptist's original disciples.[[28]](#cite_note-DrowerHaranGawaita-28)

#### Iranian religions

Several important religions and religious movements originated in [Greater Iran](/wiki/Greater_Iran "Greater Iran"), that is, among [speakers of various Iranian languages](/wiki/Iranian_peoples "Iranian peoples"). They include [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"), [Mithraism](/wiki/Mithraism "Mithraism"), [Ætsæg Din](/wiki/%C3%86ts%C3%A6g_Din "Ætsæg Din"), [Yazdanism](/wiki/Yazdanism "Yazdanism"), [Ahl-e Haqq](/wiki/Yarsanism "Yarsanism"), [Zurvanism](/wiki/Zurvanism "Zurvanism"), [Manichaeism](/wiki/Manichaeism "Manichaeism"), and [Mazdakism](/wiki/Mazdakism "Mazdakism").

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Faravahar-Gold.svg/250px-Faravahar-Gold.svg.png)

Perhaps one of the most important religions that originated in Iran was Zoroastrianism. While not, properly speaking, a world religion, it became widespread in the Iranian cultural sphere, especially through the [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") and [Sasanian](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire") Empires. It went into decline alongside many Iranian religions with the rise of Islam and the establishment of [Caliphates](/wiki/Caliphate "Caliphate"). The religion still survives today in small numbers, with a particularly notable example being the [Parsis](/wiki/Parsis "Parsis") in India and Pakistan.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30)

Scholars have often noted the similarities between Zoroastrianism and the Abrahamic religions, especially Christianity. They've particularly noted this due to the historic relationship between the Jews and the Zoroastrian Persian Empires, as well as the relationship between Greek philosophy, Persia, and Christianity. They've debated whether Zoroastrianism played an influential role in shaping these religions.

Key areas of concern include a shared sense of duality between the forces of good and evil, or light and darkness. In addition to this, there is a shared belief in the resurrection of the dead as well as an emphasis on free will and the moral responsibility of mankind. These are seen by some as having influenced the three major Abrahamic faiths as well as Gnosticism and the Baháʼí Faith.[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30)

Manichaeism is another Iranian faith that shares many similarities with Zoroastrianism. Notably, Manichaeism shares a belief in the prophethood of [Zoroaster](/wiki/Zoroaster "Zoroaster") while also acknowledging the prophethood of Gautama Buddha and Jesus Christ. It shares a dualist cosmology that pits good and light against evil and darkness, with an adversary to oppose the benevolent God.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Furthermore, Manichaeism and [Mandaeism](/wiki/Mandaeism "Mandaeism") also share a common belief in many of the figures and stories of the Abrahamic faiths, which has raised questions of influences and origins.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Indian religions

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Rigveda_MS2097.jpg/250px-Rigveda_MS2097.jpg)

In comparative religion, Indian religions refer to all the faiths that originated in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). It is believed that "the kinship of the religions of India stems from the fact that Jains, Buddhists, and Sikhs look back to Hinduism as their common mother."[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[12]](#cite_note-:3-12)

[Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni") deeply studied the Vedic religions, and through his works essential details about pre-11th century Indian religions and cultures emerged. [Adi Shankaracharya](/wiki/Adi_Shankaracharya "Adi Shankaracharya") was an early 8th century philosopher and theologian[[34]](#cite_note-34) who consolidated the doctrine of [Advaita Vedanta](/wiki/Advaita_Vedanta "Advaita Vedanta").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma1962vi-35)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEComans2000163-36)[[note 1]](#cite_note-37) [Gautama Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha") is mentioned as an [Avatar](/wiki/Avatar "Avatar") of [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu") in the [Puranic](/wiki/Puranic "Puranic") texts of Hinduism. Most Hindus believe the [Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha") accepted and incorporated many tenets of Hinduism in his doctrine.[[12]](#cite_note-:3-12) Prominent modern Hindu reformers such as [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi")[[37]](#cite_note-38) and [Vivekananda](/wiki/Vivekananda "Vivekananda")[[38]](#cite_note-39) acknowledge Buddhist influence. Like Hindus, Gandhi himself did not believe Buddha established a non-Hindu tradition. He writes, "I do not regard Jainism or Buddhism as separate from Hinduism."[[39]](#cite_note-40)

### East Asian or Taoic religions

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Tao.svg/250px-Tao.svg.png)

A Taoic religion is a religious philosophy that centers on the [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asian "East Asian") concept of *[Tao](/wiki/Tao "Tao"), meaning* "The Way". This forms a large group

--- SOURCE 5: Comparative Ethics | EBSCO Research Starters ---
URL: https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/religion-and-philosophy/comparative-ethics

SUMMARY:
Comparative ethics, also known as descriptive ethics, is a philosophical field dedicated to exploring the similarities and differences in ethical principles.

FULL CONTENT:
Looking for scholarly content to start your research? [Search EBSCOhost](https://login.ebsco.com/)

# Comparative Ethics

Comparative ethics, also known as descriptive ethics, is a philosophical field dedicated to exploring the similarities and differences in ethical principles across various societies, religions, and groups. This branch of ethics seeks to analyze moral codes, often focusing on the role of religion in shaping these codes. Given that religions significantly influence moral frameworks, comparative ethics frequently delves into comparative religious ethics, although this area can be complex due to the sensitive nature of moral discussions and the risk of misclassification.

Comparative ethics also extends to the examination of laws and constitutions in different countries, particularly regarding human rights and the rights of marginalized groups. While a relatively new and smaller field, its importance has grown with increasing global interconnectedness, raising critical questions about the validity and ethics of comparing moral systems. The field often intersects with metaethics, which considers the nature of ethics itself, prompting debates about the appropriateness and feasibility of comparing moral codes across cultures.

Despite its challenges, such as the risk of oversimplification and cultural bias, studying comparative ethics can yield valuable insights into one's own cultural norms and reveal diverse moral perspectives. Ultimately, it aims not to establish a hierarchy of moral codes but to foster a deeper understanding of varied ethical landscapes.

This branch of ethics seeks to analyze moral codes, often focusing on the role of religion in shaping these codes. Given that religions significantly influence moral frameworks, comparative ethics frequently delves into comparative religious ethics, although this area can be complex due to the sensitive nature of moral discussions and the risk of misclassification.

Comparative ethics also extends to the examination of laws and constitutions in different countries, particularly regarding human rights and the rights of marginalized groups. While a relatively new and smaller field, its importance has grown with increasing global interconnectedness, raising critical questions about the validity and ethics of comparing moral systems. The field often intersects with metaethics, which considers the nature of ethics itself, prompting debates about the appropriateness and feasibility of comparing moral codes across cultures.

Despite its challenges, such as the risk of oversimplification and cultural bias, studying comparative ethics can yield valuable insights into one's own cultural norms and reveal diverse moral perspectives. Ultimately, it aims not to establish a hierarchy of moral codes but to foster a deeper understanding of varied ethical landscapes.

## Related Topics

## On this Page

## Subject Terms

# Comparative Ethics

*Comparative ethics*, which is sometimes called descriptive ethics, is a field of study that examines similarities and differences between the ethical principles of different societies, religions, and groups of people. The study of comparative ethics is a branch of philosophy that aims to determine the best [moral principles](/research-starters/religion-and-philosophy/morality) and to discover more about the world’s various religions and moral codes.

Often, comparative ethics compares different moral codes. Since religions play such an important part in developing moral codes for various groups, comparative ethics often focuses on comparing different religions. Many comparative ethicists focus on comparative religious ethics. Because of the sensitive nature of discussing the moral codes related to the religion and the inherent classification that can occur when comparing two different things, comparative religious ethics can be a difficult subject to study and discuss.

Comparative ethics can also compare laws and constitutions of different countries to help people understand the differences and similarities between the laws and the moral codes of these different places. When comparing the rights and laws of various countries, philosophers often look at human rights issues and the rights that women and minorities have in different places.

Comparative ethics is, in relative terms, a small branch of ethical study. This is in part because comparative ethics raises difficult questions about how people can, and why they should, compare different sets of moral principles. Another reason that the field of study is somewhat small is that it is a relatively new subject. The need to compare different religions and moral structures became more necessary as the world became ever more interconnected. Before, the East and West were mostly separate because of the large physical distance, and many areas had set religions or moral standards. As technology advances, distance is not as much of a dividing factor as it once was.

## Metaethics and Comparative Ethics

[Metaethics](/research-starters/religion-and-philosophy/metaethics) is one of the three main branches of ethics: *applied ethics*, *normative ethics*, and *metaethics*. Applied ethics is the branch of ethics that examines actions that humans take and whether those actions are right or wrong. [Normative ethics](/research-starters/religion-and-philosophy/normative-ethics) tries to determine the best way for people to live. Metaethics is the study of the nature of ethics, and it examines ideas about ethics and moral reasoning. Metaethics helps philosophers and others make judgments about how to study ethics.

Philosophers often consider comparative ethics in terms of metaethics because of the nature of comparing different cultures and religions. Some philosophers question whether it is right to compare the morality of one religion with the morality of another. Others have questioned whether it is even possible to make comparisons between one society's moral code and another society's.

Although the metaethics discussion about comparative ethics will continue, enough philosophers have agreed that different moral codes and religions can be compared that the branch of comparative ethics is thriving. However, the metaethics discussion about comparing different cultures and religions has helped to change the field of comparative ethics. Current models of comparative ethics often do not identify one religion or moral code as being superior to others. Instead, many studies in comparative ethics are meant to help people better understand the religions or codes being studied. Some people who study comparative ethics also categorize the world's different religions and moral codes into groups, helping to get a better understanding.

## Drawbacks and Benefits of Comparative Ethics

Studying comparative ethics can be a difficult task. One potential problem faced by comparative ethicists is that they have to be sure they do not use ideas of a few great thinkers to generalize an entire group or culture. For example, philosopher [Immanuel Kant](/research-starters/history/immanuel-kant) is one of the most famous philosophers of the 1700s, but it would be wrong to assume all the Europeans in the 1700s shared [Kant's moral philosophy](/research-starters/history/kantian-ethics). Similarly, comparative ethicists have to make sure they do not allow their own beliefs to cloud their opinions of others. For example, the Western philosophy of the self is different from the Eastern philosophy of the self; therefore, Western philosophers have to make sure their own ideas about the self do not cloud their judgment of Eastern ideas.

Another potential problem of comparing two different moral codes is that one group may not really have a moral code. Since having a moral a code and not having one are two different things, it might not be possible to compare the morality of those two groups. Furthermore, people's desire to make comparisons could lead to their oversimplifying beliefs so they are easier to compare.

Although studying comparative ethics has its challenges, it also has a number of potential benefits. One benefit of studying comparative ethics is identifying themes in one's own culture or tradition that are not dominant but are still important. By studying other groups with different mores and rules, people can identify different moral themes and judgments.

"Comparative Religious Ethics Group." *American Academy of Religion*. American Academy of Religion. Web. 17 July 2015. https://papers.aarweb.org/content/comparative-religious-ethics-group

Hansen, Chad. "The Normative Impact of Comparative Ethics: Human Rights." *Philosophy Department*. The University of Hong Kong. 28 Oct. 1996. Web. 17 July 2015. http://www.philosophy.hku.hk/ch/Normative%20imps.htm

Stalnaker, Aaron. "Judging Others: History, Ethics, and the Purposes of Comparison." *Journal of Religious Ethics* 36 (2008): 425–444. Web. 24 July 2015. http://indiana.edu/~relstud/assets/docs/Stalnaker‗judging‗others.pdf

enriched_points.expanded_content: Comparative religious ethics studies and interprets diverse moral frameworks across religions, analyzing their ethical visions and how they guide human behavior and societal norms.
Ethical frameworks provide guidance for moral decision-making, fostering understanding and respect among diverse cultures, crucial in our interconnected global society to address ethical dilemmas.
Christianity emphasizes love, forgiveness, and moral duty, with its teachings impacting Western ethical thought and influencing global discussions on justice, charity, and human rights.
Islamic ethics, rooted in the Quran and Hadith, promotes justice, charity, and community welfare, emphasizing the importance of intention and accountability in moral actions.
Buddhism offers a unique ethical perspective focusing on compassion, non-attachment, and the alleviation of suffering, encouraging mindfulness and ethical living for personal and societal harmony.
